question: B-2. \( A \) is a subset of a finite group \( G \) (with group operation called multiplication), and \( A^{-} \)contains more than one half of the elements of \( G \). Prove that each element of \( G \) is the product of two elements of \( A \).
solution:
B-2 Let \( g \) be any element of \( G \). The set \( \left\{g a^{-1} \mid a \in A\right\} \) has the same number of elements as \( A \). If these two sets are disjoint, their union would contain more elements than \( G \). Thus there exist \( a_{1}, a_{2} \in A \) such that \( a_{1}=g a_{2}^{-1} \) and \( g=a_{1} a_{2} \).

Alternate Solution: Let \( G \) have \( n \) elements, \( A \) have \( m \) elements, and consider the multiplication table of \( G \). An element \( g \) in \( G \) must appear exactly once in each row and column of the multiplication table. It appears at most \( 2(n-m) \) times outside the table for \( A \) and \( n \) times in the table for \( G \). Thus it appears at least \( n-2(n-m)=2 m-n \) times in the table for \( A \), and we are given that \( 2 m>n \).

descriptive_long.question: B-2. \( bigseta \) is a subset of a finite group \( wholegrp \) (with group operation called multiplication), and \( bigseta^{-} \) contains more than one half of the elements of \( wholegrp \). Prove that each element of \( wholegrp \) is the product of two elements of \( bigseta \).
descriptive_long.solution:
B-2 Let \( grpelem \) be any element of \( wholegrp \). The set \( \left\{grpelem\,subelem^{-1} \mid subelem \in bigseta\right\} \) has the same number of elements as \( bigseta \). If these two sets are disjoint, their union would contain more elements than \( wholegrp \). Thus there exist \( firstsub, secondsub \in bigseta \) such that \( firstsub = grpelem\,secondsub^{-1} \) and \( grpelem = firstsub\,secondsub \).

Alternate Solution: Let \( wholegrp \) have \( groupsize \) elements, \( bigseta \) have \( subsetsz \) elements, and consider the multiplication table of \( wholegrp \). An element \( grpelem \) in \( wholegrp \) must appear exactly once in each row and column of the multiplication table. It appears at most \( 2(groupsize-subsetsz) \) times outside the table for \( bigseta \) and \( groupsize \) times in the table for \( wholegrp \). Thus it appears at least \( groupsize-2(groupsize-subsetsz)=2\,subsetsz-groupsize \) times in the table for \( bigseta \), and we are given that \( 2\,subsetsz>groupsize \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question: B-2. \( carburetor \) is a subset of a finite group \( lampshade \) (with group operation called multiplication), and \( carburetor^{-} \)contains more than one half of the elements of \( lampshade \). Prove that each element of \( lampshade \) is the product of two elements of \( carburetor \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-2 Let \( sputniks \) be any element of \( lampshade \). The set \( \left\{ sputniks pendulum^{-1} \mid pendulum \in carburetor \right\} \) has the same number of elements as \( carburetor \). If these two sets are disjoint, their union would contain more elements than \( lampshade \). Thus there exist \( pendulumone, pendulumtwo \in carburetor \) such that \( pendulumone = sputniks pendulumtwo^{-1} \) and \( sputniks = pendulumone pendulumtwo \).

Alternate Solution: Let \( lampshade \) have \( caterpillar \) elements, \( carburetor \) have \( hammocks \) elements, and consider the multiplication table of \( lampshade \). An element \( sputniks \) in \( lampshade \) must appear exactly once in each row and column of the multiplication table. It appears at most \( 2(caterpillar-hammocks) \) times outside the table for \( carburetor \) and \( caterpillar \) times in the table for \( lampshade \). Thus it appears at least \( caterpillar-2(caterpillar-hammocks)=2 hammocks-caterpillar \) times in the table for \( carburetor \), and we are given that \( 2 hammocks>caterpillar \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question: B-2. \( superset \) is a subset of a finite group \( nongroup \) (with group operation called multiplication), and \( superset^{-} \) contains more than one half of the elements of \( nongroup \). Prove that each element of \( nongroup \) is the product of two elements of \( superset \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-2 Let \( nonelement \) be any element of \( nongroup \). The set \( \left\{nonelement nonmember^{-1} \mid nonmember \in superset\right\} \) has the same number of elements as \( superset \). If these two sets are disjoint, their union would contain more elements than \( nongroup \). Thus there exist \( nonmemberone, nonmembertwo \in superset \) such that \( nonmemberone = nonelement nonmembertwo^{-1} \) and \( nonelement = nonmemberone nonmembertwo \).

Alternate Solution: Let \( nongroup \) have \( emptiness \) elements, \( superset \) have \( vastness \) elements, and consider the multiplication table of \( nongroup \). An element \( nonelement \) in \( nongroup \) must appear exactly once in each row and column of the multiplication table. It appears at most \( 2(emptiness-vastness) \) times outside the table for \( superset \) and \( emptiness \) times in the table for \( nongroup \). Thus it appears at least \( emptiness-2(emptiness-vastness)=2 vastness-emptiness \) times in the table for \( superset \), and we are given that \( 2 vastness>emptiness \).

garbled_string.question: B-2. \( qzxwvtnp \) is a subset of a finite group \( hjgrksla \) (with group operation called multiplication), and \( qzxwvtnp^{-} \) contains more than one half of the elements of \( hjgrksla \). Prove that each element of \( hjgrksla \) is the product of two elements of \( qzxwvtnp \).
garbled_string.solution:
B-2 Let \( mndtqfva \) be any element of \( hjgrksla \). The set \( \left\{mndtqfva \, rplsjkce^{-1} \mid rplsjkce \in qzxwvtnp\right\} \) has the same number of elements as \( qzxwvtnp \). If these two sets are disjoint, their union would contain more elements than \( hjgrksla \). Thus there exist \( lxqfprji, btrnsgyu \in qzxwvtnp \) such that \( lxqfprji = mndtqfva\, btrnsgyu^{-1} \) and \( mndtqfva = lxqfprji\, btrnsgyu \).

Alternate Solution: Let \( hjgrksla \) have \( vczmdhko \) elements, \( qzxwvtnp \) have \( pdylqrea \) elements, and consider the multiplication table of \( hjgrksla \). An element \( mndtqfva \) in \( hjgrksla \) must appear exactly once in each row and column of the multiplication table. It appears at most \( 2(vczmdhko-pdylqrea) \) times outside the table for \( qzxwvtnp \) and \( vczmdhko \) times in the table for \( hjgrksla \). Thus it appears at least \( vczmdhko-2(vczmdhko-pdylqrea)=2 pdylqrea-vczmdhko \) times in the table for \( qzxwvtnp \), and we are given that \( 2 pdylqrea>vczmdhko \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let $m\ge 2$ be a fixed integer and let $G$ be a finite group written multiplicatively.  
For $i=1,2,\dots ,m$ let  
\[
A_1,\;A_2,\;\dots ,\;A_m\subset G
\]
satisfy  
\[
\lvert A_i\rvert>\Bigl(1-\tfrac1m\Bigr)\lvert G\rvert \qquad\text{for every }i. \tag{$\star$}
\]

Put $n:=\lvert G\rvert$ and $B_i:=G\setminus A_i$ (so $\lvert B_i\rvert<n/m$).

1. (Surjectivity).  Prove that the $m$-fold product set  
\[
A_1A_2\cdots A_m:=\{a_1a_2\cdots a_m\mid a_i\in A_i\}
\]
equals the whole group $G$.

2. (A universal lower bound).  For $g\in G$ put  
\[
N(g):=\lvert\{(a_1,\dots ,a_m)\in A_1\times\cdots\times A_m\;:\;
a_1a_2\cdots a_m=g\}\rvert .
\]
Show that for every $g\in G$ one has  
\[
N(g)\;\ge\;\bigl(\lvert A_1\rvert+\cdots+\lvert A_m\rvert-(m-1)n\bigr)\,n^{\,m-2}. \tag{$\dagger$}
\]

3. (Equality phenomena).

   (a)  The case $m=2$.  
        Show that for $m=2$ the bound $(\dagger)$ is attained for a given
        $g\in G$ if and only if $g\notin B_1B_2$. Consequently $(\dagger)$
        holds with equality for every $g$ precisely when
        at least one of the complements $B_1,B_2$ is empty.
        (Give an explicit family of examples illustrating both possibilities.)

   (b)  The general case $m\ge 3$.  
        (i)  Prove that for any $m\ge 3$ equality in $(\dagger)$ holds for a
             fixed element $g\in G$ if and only if every solution
             $(a_1,\dots ,a_m)\in G^m$ with $a_1a_2\cdots a_m=g$
             contains at most one index $i$ with $a_i\in B_i$.  

        (ii) Deduce that if at least two of the complements $B_i$ are non-empty
             then $(\dagger)$ is strict for at least one element $g$.  

        (iii) Hence $(\dagger)$ holds with equality for every $g\in G$
              exactly in the ``degenerate'' situation where
              at most one complement $B_i$ is non-empty, i.e.\ all but possibly
              one of the sets $A_i$ coincide with $G$ itself.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout set $n:=\lvert G\rvert$ and $B_i:=G\setminus A_i$,
so that $\lvert B_i\rvert<n/m$ by $(\star)$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
1. Surjectivity of the product map  

Fix $g\in G$.

Case $m=2$.  
Because $\lvert A_1\rvert+\lvert A_2\rvert>n$,
the two sets $A_1$ and $gA_2^{-1}$ together contain
more than $n$ elements of $G$; hence they intersect.
Choose $a\in A_1\cap gA_2^{-1}$ and write $a=g a_2^{-1}$ with
$a_2\in A_2$.  Then $g=a a_2\in A_1A_2$.

Case $m\ge 3$.  
Consider all ordered $m$-tuples $(a_1,\dots ,a_m)\in G^m$
satisfying $a_1a_2\cdots a_m=g$; there are exactly $n^{\,m-1}$
of them, since the first $m-1$ coordinates can be chosen freely.
For $1\le i\le m$ put  
\[
T_i(g):=\bigl\{(a_1,\dots ,a_m)\mid a_1\cdots a_m=g,\;a_i\in B_i\bigr\}.
\]

Every ``bad'' tuple (one which violates at least one
membership condition $a_i\in A_i$) lies in the union
$T_1(g)\cup\cdots\cup T_m(g)$.

Exact counting of $T_i(g)$.  
Fix $a_i\in B_i$ (at most $n/m$ possibilities) and choose
any $m-2$ of the remaining coordinates freely
($n^{\,m-2}$ possibilities); the last coordinate is then forced.
Thus  
\[
\lvert T_i(g)\rvert=\lvert B_i\rvert\,n^{\,m-2}<\frac{n}{m}\,n^{\,m-2}
=\frac{n^{\,m-1}}{m}.
\]

Because $\sum_{i=1}^m\lvert B_i\rvert<n$ (each $\lvert B_i\rvert<n/m$),
we have  
\[
\bigl\lvert T_1(g)\cup\cdots\cup T_m(g)\bigr\rvert
\le\sum_{i=1}^m\lvert T_i(g)\rvert
=\Bigl(\sum_{i=1}^m\lvert B_i\rvert\Bigr)n^{\,m-2}<n\cdot n^{\,m-2}=n^{\,m-1}.
\]

Hence the complement of this union in the set of all solutions
is non-empty; that is, there exists an ordered $m$-tuple
$(a_1,\dots ,a_m)$ with product $g$ and \emph{all}
$a_i\in A_i$.  Consequently $g\in A_1A_2\cdots A_m$.
Since $g$ was arbitrary, we conclude $A_1A_2\cdots A_m=G$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
2. A universal lower bound for $N(g)$  

Fix $g\in G$ and keep the notation  
\[
T_i(g):=\{(a_1,\dots ,a_m)\mid a_1\cdots a_m=g,\;a_i\in B_i\}.
\]
As above,
\[
\lvert T_i(g)\rvert=\lvert B_i\rvert\,n^{\,m-2}. \tag{1}
\]

Because the family $\{T_i(g)\}_{i=1}^m$ covers precisely those
ordered solutions that violate at least one condition $a_i\in A_i$,
we have  
\[
N(g)=n^{\,m-1}-\bigl\lvert T_1(g)\cup\cdots\cup T_m(g)\bigr\rvert.
\]

Applying the union bound and (1) gives  
\[
\begin{aligned}
N(g)
&\ge n^{\,m-1}-\sum_{i=1}^m\lvert T_i(g)\rvert \\[2mm]
&=n^{\,m-1}-\Bigl(\sum_{i=1}^m\lvert B_i\rvert\Bigr)n^{\,m-2} \\[2mm]
&=\Bigl(\lvert A_1\rvert+\cdots+\lvert A_m\rvert-(m-1)n\Bigr)n^{\,m-2},
\end{aligned}
\]
establishing $(\dagger)$.
Since $(\star)$ implies the coefficient in parentheses is positive,
we also recover surjectivity.

----------------------------------------------------------------
3. Equality phenomena  

For every $g\in G$ continue to write  
\[
T_i(g):=\{(a_1,\dots ,a_m)\mid a_1\cdots a_m=g,\;a_i\in B_i\}.
\]

----------------------------------------------------------------
3(a)  The case $m=2$  

Here  
\[
T_1(g)=\{(b_1,a_2)\mid b_1\in B_1,\;b_1a_2=g\},\qquad
T_2(g)=\{(a_1,b_2)\mid b_2\in B_2,\;a_1b_2=g\}.
\]
They are disjoint exactly when there is
no solution $(b_1,b_2)$ with $b_1\in B_1$, $b_2\in B_2$ and
$b_1b_2=g$, i.e.\ precisely when $g\notin B_1B_2$.
Equality in $(\dagger)$ is equivalent to the
disjointness of $T_1(g),T_2(g)$, hence the criterion
just described.

If at least one complement is empty then $B_1B_2=\varnothing$,
so equality holds for every $g$.
Conversely, if both complements are non-empty then $B_1B_2$
certainly contains some element, giving $g$ with strict inequality.

Example.  
Let $G$ be any finite group of even order $n=2h$
possessing an index-$2$ subgroup $H$, and let
$R\subset H$ with $\lvert R\rvert=h-t$, $1\le t<h$.
Put $A_1=A_2=G\setminus R$.
Then $\lvert A_i\rvert=h+t>n/2$, so $(\star)$ holds.
For any $g$ in the coset $G\setminus H$ one checks directly that
$g\notin B_1B_2$ and that $N(g)=2t$,
equalling the right-hand side of $(\dagger)$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
3(b)  The case $m\ge 3$  

(i)  Equality criterion for one element $g$.  
Because each $\lvert T_i(g)\rvert$ has already been subtracted once,
$(\dagger)$ becomes an equality exactly when the sets
$T_1(g),\dots ,T_m(g)$ are pairwise disjoint, i.e.\ when there is
no ordered solution containing indices $i\neq j$ with
$a_i\in B_i$ \emph{and} $a_j\in B_j$.  Equivalently,
\[
(\dagger)\text{ holds with equality for }g
\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\text{every solution }(a_1,\dots ,a_m)\text{ with }
a_1\cdots a_m=g\text{ uses at most one }a_i\in B_i. \tag{$\heartsuit$}
\]

(ii)  If at least two complements are non-empty, say $B_r,B_s$,
choose $b_r\in B_r$, $b_s\in B_s$, and pick arbitrary
$a_k\in A_k$ for $k\neq r,s$.  Let  
\[
g_0:=a_1\cdots a_{r-1}\,b_r\,a_{r+1}\cdots a_{s-1}\,b_s\,a_{s+1}\cdots a_m.
\]
The displayed tuple is a solution of
$a_1\cdots a_m=g_0$ that contains two indices lying in complements,
so $(\heartsuit)$ fails for $g=g_0$ and the inequality in
$(\dagger)$ is strict for that element.

(iii)  Equality for every $g$.  
If at most one complement is non-empty,
property $(\heartsuit)$ is automatically satisfied for every $g$,
so $(\dagger)$ is always an equality.
Conversely, if two complements are non-empty then part (ii) gives
an element $g$ with strict inequality.
Hence $(\dagger)$ is tight for all $g\in G$
exactly when at most one $B_i$ is non-empty.

----------------------------------------------------------------
Remark.  
To ensure the coefficient in $(\dagger)$ is positive we only
needed the inequality
\[
\lvert A_1\rvert+\cdots+\lvert A_m\rvert
>m\Bigl(1-\tfrac1m\Bigr)n=(m-1)n,
\]
which follows immediately from $(\star)$.  No additional estimate
such as $\bigl(1-\tfrac1m\bigr)^{m}>\tfrac1m$ is required
(the latter is in fact \emph{false} for $m=3$).

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Multiple large sets:  Instead of one subset A, the problem involves an arbitrary m-tuple of different sets A₁,…,Aₘ, each near full size.  The interaction among these sets greatly complicates the combinatorics.

• Quantitative requirement:  The task is not merely to show surjectivity of the product map but also to give an explicit lower bound (†) for the number of ordered representations of every group element.  This necessitates refined counting (generalised Kövári inequality / inclusion–exclusion) far beyond the “two-set pigeonhole” argument in the original kernel.

• Extremal characterisation:  Part 3 forces the solver to identify when the lower bound is sharp and to deduce strong algebraic structure (coset decomposition and common subgroup) from equality conditions—a substantial qualitative leap.

• Advanced techniques:  The solution blends combinatorial set theory, convolution in the group ring, and structural group arguments.  None of these appear in the original problem, whose proof fits into a single easy counting step.

Together, these enhancements raise the problem well above the level of the original and current kernel variants, demanding deeper insight, heavier notation, and several distinct ideas working in concert.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $m\ge 2$ be a fixed integer and let $G$ be a finite group written multiplicatively.  
For $i=1,2,\dots ,m$ let  
\[
A_1,\;A_2,\;\dots ,\;A_m\subset G
\]
satisfy  
\[
\lvert A_i\rvert>\Bigl(1-\tfrac1m\Bigr)\lvert G\rvert \qquad\text{for every }i. \tag{$\star$}
\]

Put $n:=\lvert G\rvert$ and $B_i:=G\setminus A_i$ (so $\lvert B_i\rvert<n/m$).

1. (Surjectivity).  Prove that the $m$-fold product set  
\[
A_1A_2\cdots A_m:=\{a_1a_2\cdots a_m\mid a_i\in A_i\}
\]
equals the whole group $G$.

2. (A universal lower bound).  For $g\in G$ put  
\[
N(g):=\lvert\{(a_1,\dots ,a_m)\in A_1\times\cdots\times A_m\;:\;
a_1a_2\cdots a_m=g\}\rvert .
\]
Show that for every $g\in G$ one has  
\[
N(g)\;\ge\;\bigl(\lvert A_1\rvert+\cdots+\lvert A_m\rvert-(m-1)n\bigr)\,n^{\,m-2}. \tag{$\dagger$}
\]

3. (Equality phenomena).

   (a)  The case $m=2$.  
        Show that for $m=2$ the bound $(\dagger)$ is attained for a given
        $g\in G$ if and only if $g\notin B_1B_2$. Consequently $(\dagger)$
        holds with equality for every $g$ precisely when
        at least one of the complements $B_1,B_2$ is empty.
        (Give an explicit family of examples illustrating both possibilities.)

   (b)  The general case $m\ge 3$.  
        (i)  Prove that for any $m\ge 3$ equality in $(\dagger)$ holds for a
             fixed element $g\in G$ if and only if every solution
             $(a_1,\dots ,a_m)\in G^m$ with $a_1a_2\cdots a_m=g$
             contains at most one index $i$ with $a_i\in B_i$.  

        (ii) Deduce that if at least two of the complements $B_i$ are non-empty
             then $(\dagger)$ is strict for at least one element $g$.  

        (iii) Hence $(\dagger)$ holds with equality for every $g\in G$
              exactly in the ``degenerate'' situation where
              at most one complement $B_i$ is non-empty, i.e.\ all but possibly
              one of the sets $A_i$ coincide with $G$ itself.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout set $n:=\lvert G\rvert$ and $B_i:=G\setminus A_i$,
so that $\lvert B_i\rvert<n/m$ by $(\star)$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
1. Surjectivity of the product map  

Fix $g\in G$.

Case $m=2$.  
Because $\lvert A_1\rvert+\lvert A_2\rvert>n$,
the two sets $A_1$ and $gA_2^{-1}$ together contain
more than $n$ elements of $G$; hence they intersect.
Choose $a\in A_1\cap gA_2^{-1}$ and write $a=g a_2^{-1}$ with
$a_2\in A_2$.  Then $g=a a_2\in A_1A_2$.

Case $m\ge 3$.  
Consider all ordered $m$-tuples $(a_1,\dots ,a_m)\in G^m$
satisfying $a_1a_2\cdots a_m=g$; there are exactly $n^{\,m-1}$
of them, since the first $m-1$ coordinates can be chosen freely.
For $1\le i\le m$ put  
\[
T_i(g):=\bigl\{(a_1,\dots ,a_m)\mid a_1\cdots a_m=g,\;a_i\in B_i\bigr\}.
\]

Every ``bad'' tuple (one which violates at least one
membership condition $a_i\in A_i$) lies in the union
$T_1(g)\cup\cdots\cup T_m(g)$.

Exact counting of $T_i(g)$.  
Fix $a_i\in B_i$ (at most $n/m$ possibilities) and choose
any $m-2$ of the remaining coordinates freely
($n^{\,m-2}$ possibilities); the last coordinate is then forced.
Thus  
\[
\lvert T_i(g)\rvert=\lvert B_i\rvert\,n^{\,m-2}<\frac{n}{m}\,n^{\,m-2}
=\frac{n^{\,m-1}}{m}.
\]

Because $\sum_{i=1}^m\lvert B_i\rvert<n$ (each $\lvert B_i\rvert<n/m$),
we have  
\[
\bigl\lvert T_1(g)\cup\cdots\cup T_m(g)\bigr\rvert
\le\sum_{i=1}^m\lvert T_i(g)\rvert
=\Bigl(\sum_{i=1}^m\lvert B_i\rvert\Bigr)n^{\,m-2}<n\cdot n^{\,m-2}=n^{\,m-1}.
\]

Hence the complement of this union in the set of all solutions
is non-empty; that is, there exists an ordered $m$-tuple
$(a_1,\dots ,a_m)$ with product $g$ and \emph{all}
$a_i\in A_i$.  Consequently $g\in A_1A_2\cdots A_m$.
Since $g$ was arbitrary, we conclude $A_1A_2\cdots A_m=G$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
2. A universal lower bound for $N(g)$  

Fix $g\in G$ and keep the notation  
\[
T_i(g):=\{(a_1,\dots ,a_m)\mid a_1\cdots a_m=g,\;a_i\in B_i\}.
\]
As above,
\[
\lvert T_i(g)\rvert=\lvert B_i\rvert\,n^{\,m-2}. \tag{1}
\]

Because the family $\{T_i(g)\}_{i=1}^m$ covers precisely those
ordered solutions that violate at least one condition $a_i\in A_i$,
we have  
\[
N(g)=n^{\,m-1}-\bigl\lvert T_1(g)\cup\cdots\cup T_m(g)\bigr\rvert.
\]

Applying the union bound and (1) gives  
\[
\begin{aligned}
N(g)
&\ge n^{\,m-1}-\sum_{i=1}^m\lvert T_i(g)\rvert \\[2mm]
&=n^{\,m-1}-\Bigl(\sum_{i=1}^m\lvert B_i\rvert\Bigr)n^{\,m-2} \\[2mm]
&=\Bigl(\lvert A_1\rvert+\cdots+\lvert A_m\rvert-(m-1)n\Bigr)n^{\,m-2},
\end{aligned}
\]
establishing $(\dagger)$.
Since $(\star)$ implies the coefficient in parentheses is positive,
we also recover surjectivity.

----------------------------------------------------------------
3. Equality phenomena  

For every $g\in G$ continue to write  
\[
T_i(g):=\{(a_1,\dots ,a_m)\mid a_1\cdots a_m=g,\;a_i\in B_i\}.
\]

----------------------------------------------------------------
3(a)  The case $m=2$  

Here  
\[
T_1(g)=\{(b_1,a_2)\mid b_1\in B_1,\;b_1a_2=g\},\qquad
T_2(g)=\{(a_1,b_2)\mid b_2\in B_2,\;a_1b_2=g\}.
\]
They are disjoint exactly when there is
no solution $(b_1,b_2)$ with $b_1\in B_1$, $b_2\in B_2$ and
$b_1b_2=g$, i.e.\ precisely when $g\notin B_1B_2$.
Equality in $(\dagger)$ is equivalent to the
disjointness of $T_1(g),T_2(g)$, hence the criterion
just described.

If at least one complement is empty then $B_1B_2=\varnothing$,
so equality holds for every $g$.
Conversely, if both complements are non-empty then $B_1B_2$
certainly contains some element, giving $g$ with strict inequality.

Example.  
Let $G$ be any finite group of even order $n=2h$
possessing an index-$2$ subgroup $H$, and let
$R\subset H$ with $\lvert R\rvert=h-t$, $1\le t<h$.
Put $A_1=A_2=G\setminus R$.
Then $\lvert A_i\rvert=h+t>n/2$, so $(\star)$ holds.
For any $g$ in the coset $G\setminus H$ one checks directly that
$g\notin B_1B_2$ and that $N(g)=2t$,
equalling the right-hand side of $(\dagger)$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
3(b)  The case $m\ge 3$  

(i)  Equality criterion for one element $g$.  
Because each $\lvert T_i(g)\rvert$ has already been subtracted once,
$(\dagger)$ becomes an equality exactly when the sets
$T_1(g),\dots ,T_m(g)$ are pairwise disjoint, i.e.\ when there is
no ordered solution containing indices $i\neq j$ with
$a_i\in B_i$ \emph{and} $a_j\in B_j$.  Equivalently,
\[
(\dagger)\text{ holds with equality for }g
\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\text{every solution }(a_1,\dots ,a_m)\text{ with }
a_1\cdots a_m=g\text{ uses at most one }a_i\in B_i. \tag{$\heartsuit$}
\]

(ii)  If at least two complements are non-empty, say $B_r,B_s$,
choose $b_r\in B_r$, $b_s\in B_s$, and pick arbitrary
$a_k\in A_k$ for $k\neq r,s$.  Let  
\[
g_0:=a_1\cdots a_{r-1}\,b_r\,a_{r+1}\cdots a_{s-1}\,b_s\,a_{s+1}\cdots a_m.
\]
The displayed tuple is a solution of
$a_1\cdots a_m=g_0$ that contains two indices lying in complements,
so $(\heartsuit)$ fails for $g=g_0$ and the inequality in
$(\dagger)$ is strict for that element.

(iii)  Equality for every $g$.  
If at most one complement is non-empty,
property $(\heartsuit)$ is automatically satisfied for every $g$,
so $(\dagger)$ is always an equality.
Conversely, if two complements are non-empty then part (ii) gives
an element $g$ with strict inequality.
Hence $(\dagger)$ is tight for all $g\in G$
exactly when at most one $B_i$ is non-empty.

----------------------------------------------------------------
Remark.  
To ensure the coefficient in $(\dagger)$ is positive we only
needed the inequality
\[
\lvert A_1\rvert+\cdots+\lvert A_m\rvert
>m\Bigl(1-\tfrac1m\Bigr)n=(m-1)n,
\]
which follows immediately from $(\star)$.  No additional estimate
such as $\bigl(1-\tfrac1m\bigr)^{m}>\tfrac1m$ is required
(the latter is in fact \emph{false} for $m=3$).

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Multiple large sets:  Instead of one subset A, the problem involves an arbitrary m-tuple of different sets A₁,…,Aₘ, each near full size.  The interaction among these sets greatly complicates the combinatorics.

• Quantitative requirement:  The task is not merely to show surjectivity of the product map but also to give an explicit lower bound (†) for the number of ordered representations of every group element.  This necessitates refined counting (generalised Kövári inequality / inclusion–exclusion) far beyond the “two-set pigeonhole” argument in the original kernel.

• Extremal characterisation:  Part 3 forces the solver to identify when the lower bound is sharp and to deduce strong algebraic structure (coset decomposition and common subgroup) from equality conditions—a substantial qualitative leap.

• Advanced techniques:  The solution blends combinatorial set theory, convolution in the group ring, and structural group arguments.  None of these appear in the original problem, whose proof fits into a single easy counting step.

Together, these enhancements raise the problem well above the level of the original and current kernel variants, demanding deeper insight, heavier notation, and several distinct ideas working in concert.
